elements.sent-2411-661: To show off the effectiveness and the generalization of proposals, the thesis considers three baselines as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-662: PPO, which stands for Proximal Policy Optimization algorithm \cite{schulman2017proximal , is among the most popular and effective techniques for single-agent reinforcement learning.}
elements.sent-2411-663: While the original PPO is not originally designed for the multi-agent setting and asynchronous decision-making environments, this thesis has adapted it to suit the Dec-POSMDP formulation.
elements.sent-2411-664: The data collection and parameter update process remains the same as in AMAPPO.
elements.sent-2411-665: However, in the PPO algorithm, only one actor and critic are employed to govern the operations of multiple mobile chargers.
elements.sent-2411-666: IPPO: The Independent Proximal Policy Optimization algorithm \cite{yu2022surprising is a modified version of PPO specifically designed to address the multi-agent setting.}
elements.sent-2411-667: In this algorithm, each mobile charger is treated as an independent learner, with its own actor and critic.
elements.sent-2411-668: Consequently, each mobile charger collects its own samples, which are then utilized to train its respective PPO model.
elements.sent-2411-669: This approach allows for individualized learning and decision-making by each mobile charger.
elements.sent-2411-670: DTCM: The Distributed charging scheme under Target coverage and Connectivity constraint for Multiple chargers is a reinforcement learning-based algorithm designed to solve the target coverage and connectivity in WRSNs.
elements.sent-2411-671: The algorithm implements a Q-learning algorithm on their proposed MDP model whose action space is traveling to a predefined charging location.
elements.sent-2411-672: The cooperation between mobile chargers is ensured by a consensus mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-673: The objective of the thesis is to maximize the network lifetime which is calculated through Formula~\ref{eq:lifetime and the network simulation.}
elements.sent-2411-674: However, due to the physical geometry characteristic of each network scenario being different, the thesis adopts an alternate metric called network lifetime improvement, calculated as follows: \begin{equation}\text{Network lifetime improvement} = \dfrac{F_0}{F_B},\end{equation} where $F_0$ is the network lifetime when implementing mobile chargers with a certain controlling algorithm, while $F_B$ is the network lifetime without using mobile chargers.
elements.sent-2411-675: With this metric, an inferior algorithm would result in the network lifetime improvement of 1 while a good algorithm aims to increase the improvement as high as possible.
elements.sent-2411-676: The thesis introduces a real-world dataset comprising 9 instances, derived from the geographical information of Vietnam.
elements.sent-2411-677: Each instance consists of the location of a base station and a set of targets.
elements.sent-2411-678: The base station serves as an information center, while the targets are strategically placed objects of interest.
elements.sent-2411-679: The instances are named following a convention of "province\_no.
elements.sent-2411-680: The "No. targets" taken from the set \{50, 100, 150, 200\}, denotes the number of targets to be monitored in each instance.
elements.sent-2411-681: For example, the instance "hanoi\_50" represents a scenario where the base station is situated at the Vietnam National University of Agriculture, and there are 50 targets that serve as transportation stations, illustrated in Figure~\ref{fig: hanoi_50_target .}
elements.sent-2411-682: To ensure effective target monitoring and connection to the base station, sensors are strategically positioned within each instance, following the approach outlined in the paper \cite{HANH2023103578 .}
elements.sent-2411-683: Each instance comprises the locations of sensors, targets, and the base station.
elements.sent-2411-684: The instance "hanoi\_50" with the sensor placement is illustrated in Figure~\ref{fig: hanoi_50_target_sensor_placement .}
elements.sent-2411-685: Throughout the training and testing phases, the number of mobile chargers is fixed at 3.
elements.sent-2411-686: The training of AMAPPO and other reinforcement learning algorithms is conducted using the "hanoi\_50" instance in a simulation environment, while the other instances serve as evaluation maps to assess the testing performance.
elements.sent-2411-687: To showcase the importance of each component in the Dec-POSMDP formulation and the AMAPPO algorithm, this section provides a comprehensive set of ablation experiments.
elements.sent-2411-688: These experiments involve various ablation variants of the proposed methods, which are carefully designed to isolate the effects of different components.
elements.sent-2411-689: The performance of all ablation variants is summarized in Table~\ref{tab: ablation1 .}
elements.sent-2411-690: Additionally, in the following subsections, we provide detailed insights into the performance of each group of variants throughout the training progress.
elements.sent-2411-691: This analysis highlights the significance of different components in achieving efficient and cooperative charging strategies for the MCs, ultimately improving the network lifetime and overall system performance.

elements.subsection-2411-010: The importance of observation space's components
elements.figure-2411-024: [H] \includegraphics[width= \linewidth]{fig1.pdf} \caption{Ablation study on components of observation space} \label{fig:ob_ablation_study}
elements.sent-2411-692: This experiment highlights the significance of different components in the observation space on the learning process and performance of mobile chargers.
elements.sent-2411-693: The experiment evaluates three variants of the observation space: "FULL," which represents the proposed observation space in the thesis, "NO\_1" excluding the first layer, and "NO\_2\_3\_4" containing only the first layer as the observation space.
elements.sent-2411-694: The network lifetime improvement for these variants over the training phase is illustrated in Figure~\ref{fig:ob_ablation_study .}
elements.sent-2411-695: Overall, the AMAPPO trained on the "FULL" variant outperforms that trained on the "NO\_2\_3\_4" and "NO\_1" variants.
elements.sent-2411-696: The first layer of the observation space places emphasis on areas with multiple sensors in critical conditions, particularly those with low levels of energy and high consumption rates.
elements.sent-2411-697: Sensors consuming substantial energy are responsible for numerous tasks in the network.
elements.sent-2411-698: Hence, when these sensors cease operation, it often leads to the disconnection of important network components.
elements.sent-2411-699: Consequently, removing the first layer significantly hampers the learning process, as mobile chargers are unable to recognize critical network conditions.
elements.sent-2411-700: This is evident from the fixed network lifetime improvement around 1.0, indicating no enhancement during training.
elements.sent-2411-701: In the "NO\_2\_3\_4" variant, where the information about the status of mobile chargers in the second, third, and fourth layers is ignored, the learning process still shows some positive results.
elements.sent-2411-702: However, the performance is much less competitive compared to the full version.
elements.sent-2411-703: Moreover, this variant exhibits oscillations and multiple sudden decreases during training.
elements.sent-2411-704: This phenomenon can be attributed to the lack of information about other mobile chargers in the decision-making process.
elements.sent-2411-705: Specifically, the first layer still guides mobile chargers to critical areas, primarily contributing to the enhancement of network lifetime.
elements.sent-2411-706: However, since mobile chargers are unaware of each other's intentions, they might inadvertently visit the same area, resulting in insufficient energy replenishment for other critical areas.
elements.sent-2411-707: This demonstrates that incorporating the information of other mobile chargers through the three layers facilitates cooperation between mobile chargers, leading to more efficient network lifetime improvement.
elements.sent-2411-708: This section presents a detailed analysis of the general reward and the exclusive reward of mobile chargers (MCs).
elements.sent-2411-709: We implement three variants to compare the separate impact of each reward term: the "FULL" variant represents the proposed combined reward function, the "NO\_EX" variant indicates the reward function without the exclusive term, and the "NO\_GE" variant represents the version excluding the general term.
elements.sent-2411-710: The change in network lifetime improvement for these three variants during the training progress is illustrated in Figure~\ref{fig:ab_reward .}
elements.sent-2411-711: Overall, the performance of all three variants improves during training.
elements.sent-2411-712: However, the "FULL" and "NO\_GE" variants demonstrate better performance when considering the cutoff at three million training frames.
elements.sent-2411-713: The "NO\_GE" version achieves a comparable enhancement in network lifetime to the full version, despite lacking the general term in its reward function.
elements.sent-2411-714: This could be attributed to the fact that the exclusive term already captures a portion of the general term when distributing the total exclusive rewards among MCs charging the same sensors.
elements.sent-2411-715: This encourages MCs to avoid visiting the proximity of others, leading to efficient resource utilization.
elements.sent-2411-716: However, the "NO\_GE" variant does not as competitive in the testing instances as the "FULL" variants.
elements.sent-2411-717: This highlights the vital role of the general reward components in creating a general multi-agent system.
elements.sent-2411-718: On the other hand, when removing the exclusive term in the "NO\_EX" variant, the phenomenon of "lazy agents" becomes apparent, which is a common occurrence in multi-agent systems with joint reward functions.
elements.sent-2411-719: This phenomenon arises from the overvaluation of certain actions by one or more agents, even though the overall benefit is contributed by other agents.
elements.sent-2411-720: As a result, this leads to high oscillations in performance during training, hindering cooperation and overall performance improvement.

elements.sent-2411-724: In essence, the space of the charging location is large and includes multiple no-reward signal areas.
elements.sent-2411-725: Hence, it is demanding to train agents to make decisions directly in this space.
elements.sent-2411-726: The exploration just brings agents to a proximal area that is impossible to escape from the no-reward signal area.

elements.sent-2411-728: In this experiment, the thesis aims to clarify the vital role of the architecture of AMAPPO through comparison with two other architectures, including IPPO and PPO.
elements.sent-2411-729: Regarding IPPO, this algorithm considers each mobile charger as an independent learner, so implementing an actor and a critic for each mobile charger.
elements.sent-2411-730: The PPO algorithm adopts only one actor and critic to regulate the operation of multiple mobile chargers.
elements.sent-2411-731: For a fair comparison, all three algorithms utilize the same architecture of actor in Section~\ref{sec: actor_model , critic in Section~\ref{sec: critic_model , and also the learning process in Algorithm~\ref{al:amappo .}}}
elements.sent-2411-732: The only differences are the weight update and action-making that depend on which actor and critic is responsible for each actor.
elements.sent-2411-733: Overall, the proposed architecture showcases a better performance than the two counterparts.
elements.sent-2411-734: Specifically, after three million training frames, the final models of AMAPPO show a higher gap of 0.21 and 0.59 in the network lifetime improvement than IPPO, and PPO, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-735: It is worth noting that the IPPO algorithm performance is unstable during training which is confirmed by multiple sudden drops in network lifetime improvement and extremely high oscillation.
elements.sent-2411-736: This would be explained by the architecture separating critics, which slower the training of the critic models, and even leads to the significant difference between should-be-the-same critic evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-737: The slow update of the critic model is evident when actors reach some highly better policies.
elements.sent-2411-738: In this context, the critic model is not prompt to shift the parameters to have a good evaluation for these better behaviors that have not been seen before.

elements.sent-2411-739: This experiment aims to evaluate the network lifetime improvement of pre-trained models on testing instances.
elements.sent-2411-740: To enhance clarity, instances with the same number of targets are grouped together, and the average value is computed for each group.
elements.sent-2411-741: The results for each model group are presented in Figure~\ref{fig: testing_performance .}
elements.sent-2411-742: As a general trend, the network lifetime improvement shows a decreasing pattern with an increase in the number of targets.
elements.sent-2411-743: This phenomenon is expected, as a higher number of targets necessitates more sensors for surveillance and data transmission, leading to higher energy demand on the network.
elements.sent-2411-744: Additionally, the increased number of targets poses a greater risk of losing connection to one or more targets, further contributing to the decrease in network lifetime improvement.
elements.sent-2411-745: The DTCM algorithm stands out as the only one using a distinct formulation, while IPPO, PPO, and AMAPPO all rely on the proposed DEC-POSMDP formulation.
elements.sent-2411-746: Due to the specific nature of the DTCM formulation, it cannot be directly applied to different instances apart from the training instance.
elements.sent-2411-747: Therefore, for the testing phase, it becomes crucial to retrain DTCM on a new formulation specific to each instance.
elements.sent-2411-748: In contrast, the proposed DEC-POSMDP formulation exhibits generalization capabilities, enabling pre-trained models of AMAPPO, IPPO, and PPO to effectively operate in various network instances, even if they were trained on a different instance.
elements.sent-2411-749: Moreover, the performance of AMAPPO and IPPO is still much more remarkable in testing instances.
elements.sent-2411-750: Specifically, AMAPPO can averagely lengthen the network lifetime up to more than three times for 50-target instances, and 2.15 times in overall.
elements.sent-2411-751: The performance of IPPO reaches the rate of 1.85 in overall.
elements.sent-2411-752: As mentioned above, DTCM must retrain its model, which likely causes the death of the network before the model can learn something significant, which is indicated by its inferior network lifetime improvement of 1.1 overall.
elements.sent-2411-753: To highlight the performance of AMAPPO and IPPO, an additional model of DTCM, named DTCM(1M), is trained for each testing instance in 1 million frames.
elements.sent-2411-754: Indeed, this version of DTCM has a more favorable condition to reach an excellent performance due to its already access to the data from testing instances.
elements.sent-2411-755: However, this version only has performance better than the pre-trained PPO model but is less competitive with AMAPPO and IPPO, which do not know any information about testing instances.
elements.sent-2411-756: This comparison showcases the significant generalization and effectiveness of the proposed DEC-POSMDP formulation.
elements.sent-2411-757: Regarding the proposed algorithm, AMAPPO outperforms its counterparts in all instances, showing a network lifetime improvement up to 3.13, 2.35, 1.69, and 1.43 for instances with 50, 100, 150, and 200 targets, respectively.

elements.sent-2411-758: It introduces a fleet of mobile chargers with a multi-node charging model to achieve this goal.
elements.sent-2411-759: A key contribution is the development of a general Dec-POSMDP formulation for NTM-CTC, allowing mobile chargers to perform effectively without the need for retraining across diverse network scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-760: To address the synchronous nature of the Dec-POSMDP formulation, an asynchronous multi-agent learning framework is proposed, enabling the application of state-of-the-art reinforcement learning algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-761: The final proposed algorithm, AMAPPO, is a modified version of the PPO algorithm tailored to the Dec-POSMDP formulation of NTM-CTC.
elements.sent-2411-762: To evaluate the performance, a novel training-testing procedure is introduced using a dataset with Vietnam's geometry information, representing various real-world scenarios for NTM-CTC.
elements.sent-2411-763: A series of ablation studies are conducted to demonstrate the individual contributions of each component in the proposed approaches.
elements.sent-2411-764: The numerical results highlight the superior performance of AMAPPO compared to other methods.
elements.sent-2411-765: In conclusion, the thesis paves the way for a new research direction, focusing on the creation of general agents that accumulate knowledge over time and can adapt to various environments.
elements.sent-2411-766: The proposed framework and algorithm showcase promising advancements in maximizing network lifetime and target coverage in wireless sensor networks.
elements.sent-2411-767: Limitation Despite its significant contributions, the thesis does face two primary limitations.
elements.sent-2411-768: Firstly, there is a lack of investigation into the system's flexibility when the number of mobile chargers is dynamically increased or decreased.
elements.sent-2411-769: Understanding how the system adapts to changes in the number of agents is crucial for real-world applicability.
elements.sent-2411-770: Secondly, the proposal's robustness against environmental uncertainties, such as anomaly events impacting target behavior and sensor energy burden, remains unexplored.
elements.sent-2411-771: Evaluating the proposal's performance under varying and uncertain conditions is important for assessing its practicality and reliability.
elements.sent-2411-772: Future work For future work, the thesis aims to develop an extension mechanism for the multi-agent system, allowing seamless integration of new agents with reasonable parameters and proper regularization of existing agents when agents are removed.
elements.sent-2411-773: This mechanism is vital in real-world scenarios to ensure continuous performance despite changes in the agent composition.
elements.sent-2411-774: Additionally, the thesis plans to conduct experiments to analyze the proposal's robustness under different levels of environmental randomness.
elements.sent-2411-775: Understanding how the proposal performs in uncertain and dynamic environments will provide valuable insights into its applicability and potential areas of improvement.
elements.sent-2411-776: Addressing these limitations and exploring these future directions will further enhance the effectiveness and practicality of the proposed approach in real-world applications.
